site.title: Kernel Engineering
site.tagline: From silicon to speculative decoding — write GPU kernels that actually run modern LLMs.

sections.title: Start Here
sections.blurb: The mental models. Why kernels decide who wins, how to think in speed-of-light terms, and the exact skill map a GPU kernel engineer is hired for.

articles.title: Why kernels run the world
articles.blurb: Every frontier model is, at the bottom, a pile of hand-tuned kernels. Here is why that layer is the one worth learning.

articles.title: The three regimes: compute, memory, overhead
articles.blurb: The single most useful mental model in performance: every kernel is bound by compute, memory bandwidth, or overhead. Learn to tell which.

articles.title: Speed-of-light thinking & the roofline
articles.blurb: Arithmetic intensity, peak FLOPs vs peak bytes, and the roofline that tells you the fastest your kernel could ever be.

articles.title: The kernel engineer's skill map
articles.blurb: What hiring managers actually ask for — matmul-from-scratch to CUTLASS to AI-generated kernels — and where this site teaches each.

articles.title: How to use this site
articles.blurb: The two surfaces, the worklog style, the async track, and the order to read things in.

sections.title: The GPU, From Silicon Up
sections.blurb: A tour of the H100/B200 from the die down to the wire. Every component, what it costs, and why it exists — the vocabulary the rest of the site assumes.

articles.title: The Streaming Multiprocessor
articles.blurb: The unit of GPU compute. Sub-partitions, schedulers, and the on-chip memory that makes or breaks a kernel.

articles.title: CUDA cores & the FP32/INT pipes
articles.blurb: The scalar ALUs, what they can and can't do, and why they are no longer where the FLOPs are.

articles.title: Tensor cores
articles.blurb: The matrix-multiply-accumulate engines that hold ~95% of an H100's FLOPs. Shapes, precisions, and why kernels are written around them.

articles.title: The warp scheduler & latency hiding
articles.blurb: How an SM hides 400-cycle memory latency by juggling dozens of warps — the reason occupancy matters.

articles.title: The register file
articles.blurb: The fastest and most contended memory on the chip. Register pressure, spills, and why 255 is a magic number.

articles.title: Shared memory & L1
articles.blurb: The programmable scratchpad that co-lives with L1. 228 KiB, 32 banks, and the single most important optimization surface.

articles.title: The L2 cache & partitions
articles.blurb: 60 MiB split across two partitions with a crossbar, data compression, residency control, and near/far latency.

articles.title: HBM, global memory & the package
articles.blurb: Stacked DRAM, through-silicon vias, the interposer, 3.35 TB/s — and why every kernel is ultimately a memory-traffic problem.

articles.title: GPCs, TPCs & the chip floorplan
articles.blurb: How 132 SMs are grouped, why defective SMs get fused off, and what thread-block clusters buy you.

articles.title: A100 → H100 → B200: what changed
articles.blurb: TMA, wgmma, DSMEM, FP8, then tcgen05, TMEM and NVFP4 — the architectural deltas that each spawned new kernels.

articles.title: The roofline model in practice
articles.blurb: Plotting your kernel on the roofline, reading the ridge point, and knowing when to stop optimizing.

articles.title: Occupancy: the balancing act
articles.blurb: Registers × threads × shared memory → how many warps fit, and why more occupancy is not always faster.

articles.title: Arithmetic intensity
articles.blurb: FLOPs per byte: the number that decides your regime and the target of every tiling trick.

articles.title: Memory coalescing
articles.blurb: How a warp's 32 loads become one 128-byte transaction — or thirty-two — and what that does to your bandwidth.

sections.title: The CUDA Programming Model
sections.blurb: From the abstract launch to the metal. Threads to grids, the compilation story, and the primitives you build every kernel out of.

articles.title: Threads, warps, blocks, grids
articles.blurb: The execution hierarchy and how it maps onto SMs, plus the indexing arithmetic you will write a thousand times.

articles.title: SIMT & warp divergence
articles.blurb: Why 32 threads share a program counter, what happens at an if-statement, and how divergence quietly halves your throughput.

articles.title: Anatomy of a kernel launch
articles.blurb: Grid/block dims, launch overhead, streams, and what actually happens between <<<>>> and the first instruction.

articles.title: The memory spaces
articles.blurb: Global, shared, local, constant, register, texture — the full map, with latencies and when to reach for each.

articles.title: PTX vs SASS: the compilation story
articles.blurb: nvcc → PTX → ptxas → SASS. What is virtual, what is real, and why you read SASS to find the truth.

articles.title: Compute capability & targeting
articles.blurb: sm_90a and friends — how to compile for the right architecture and why the 'a' matters on Hopper.

articles.title: Shared-memory bank conflicts
articles.blurb: 32 banks, the conflict rule, and the padding/swizzling fixes that recover the bandwidth you paid for.

articles.title: Atomics & reductions
articles.blurb: Warp-shuffle reductions, atomicAdd, and how to sum a million numbers without serializing your GPU.

articles.title: Streams, events & async
articles.blurb: Overlapping copy and compute, cp.async, and the concurrency that keeps the SMs fed.

articles.title: Your first kernel, end to end
articles.blurb: SAXPY to RGB→grayscale: launch config, boundary checks, and benchmarking done right, from scratch.

articles.title: GPU Puzzles, walkthrough I
articles.blurb: Solving the first set of Sasha Rush's GPU Puzzles — map, zip, broadcast, and the mental model of one-thread-one-element.

articles.title: GPU Puzzles, walkthrough II
articles.blurb: Pooling, dot product, convolution and prefix sum — where shared memory and cooperation enter the picture.

sections.title: The GEMM Worklog
sections.blurb: The heart of the course. We rebuild matrix multiply from a 1.3%-of-cuBLAS naive kernel to a 94% warptiled monster — one optimization, one measurement, one figure at a time. Then we do it again on tensor cores.

articles.title: Kernel 1: Naive
articles.blurb: One thread per output element. The baseline, the profile, and why it leaves 98% of the GPU on the table.

articles.title: Kernel 2: Global memory coalescing
articles.blurb: A one-line remap of thread indices that turns 32 scattered loads into one — a 6× win for free.

articles.title: Kernel 3: Shared-memory tiling
articles.blurb: Blocking over K, staging tiles in SMEM, and cutting global-memory traffic by the tile width.

articles.title: Kernel 4: 1D block-tiling
articles.blurb: One thread computes many outputs. Register caching pushes arithmetic intensity past the memory wall.

articles.title: Kernel 5: 2D block-tiling
articles.blurb: A 2D grid of results per thread. Input reuse in registers doubles us again.

articles.title: Kernel 6: Vectorized memory access
articles.blurb: float4 / LDS.128 loads. The SASS moment where 8 instructions collapse into 2.

articles.title: Kernel 7: Autotuning the tiles
articles.blurb: BM, BN, BK, TM, TN — searching the parameter space instead of guessing it.

articles.title: Kernel 8: Warptiling
articles.blurb: Making every level of parallelism explicit — block, warp, thread — to reach cuBLAS territory.

articles.title: Double buffering & cp.async
articles.blurb: Overlapping the next tile's load with this tile's math to hide the memory latency entirely.

articles.title: What cuBLAS is actually doing
articles.blurb: The black box we've been chasing: heuristics, split-K, and why the last 6% is so hard.

articles.title: Benchmarking without lying to yourself
articles.blurb: Warmup, clocks, L2 flushing, and reporting TFLOP/s honestly — the discipline that makes the whole ladder trustworthy.

articles.title: The ladder, end to end
articles.blurb: All ten kernels on one roofline, the speedups stacked, and the general principles that transfer to any kernel.

articles.title: Tensor cores I: the WMMA GEMM
articles.blurb: Rebuilding matmul on tensor cores from scratch (after alexarmbr): fragments, the mma shape, and a first working kernel.

articles.title: Tensor cores II: fragments & swizzling
articles.blurb: Register-fragment layouts, SMEM swizzling to kill bank conflicts on the tensor-core path, and the profile that proves it.

articles.title: Tensor cores III: to cuBLAS speed
articles.blurb: mma.sync, double-buffered pipelines and the precision menu — a tensor-core GEMM that rivals the library.

sections.title: Kernels for Inference
sections.blurb: Where the GEMM skills meet real LLMs. Fusion, softmax, attention, FlashAttention, the KV cache and the quantized kernels that serve tokens at scale.

articles.title: Prefill vs decode: two different machines
articles.blurb: Why the compute-bound prefill (GEMM) and the memory-bound decode (GEMV) demand completely different kernels.

articles.title: Operator fusion
articles.blurb: The highest-leverage inference optimization: stop round-tripping tensors through HBM between elementwise ops.

articles.title: Softmax from scratch (and online)
articles.blurb: The numerically-stable, single-pass, online softmax — the trick that makes FlashAttention possible.

articles.title: RMSNorm & LayerNorm kernels
articles.blurb: Reduction + normalize + affine, fused into one pass — the workhorse normalization of modern LLMs.

articles.title: Attention, the naive way
articles.blurb: QKᵀ → softmax → V as three kernels, its O(N²) HBM traffic, and why that is the problem to solve.

articles.title: FlashAttention I: tiling attention
articles.blurb: Fusing the whole attention into one kernel with online softmax so the N×N scores never touch HBM.

articles.title: FlashAttention II: better work partitioning
articles.blurb: Rebalancing work across warps and the sequence dimension for a ~2× step over FA1.

articles.title: FlashAttention III: Hopper & async
articles.blurb: Warp specialization, TMA and FP8 on Hopper — attention at the hardware's speed of light.

articles.title: The KV cache & PagedAttention
articles.blurb: Why decode is memory-bound, how paging fixes fragmentation, and the kernel that reads a paged cache.

articles.title: Quantization kernels: FP8, INT4, W4A16
articles.blurb: Dequantize-in-the-kernel, scale handling, and the low-precision matmuls that halve or quarter memory traffic.

articles.title: The SwiGLU kernel
articles.blurb: Gate × up × SiLU fused — a small kernel that shows up in every transformer MLP and every kernel benchmark.

articles.title: Batched decode: the GEMV problem
articles.blurb: Serving many sequences one token at a time: skinny matmuls, memory-bound reality, and how batching helps.

sections.title: The Frontier
sections.blurb: The cutting edge, as of now. Hopper's async engine, Blackwell's tensor memory and NVFP4, DeepSeek's open kernels, CUTLASS the hard way, and how to debug when it all breaks.

articles.title: Hopper's TMA: async bulk copy
articles.blurb: The Tensor Memory Accelerator: hardware-managed multidimensional copies that free the threads to compute.

articles.title: WGMMA & warp specialization
articles.blurb: Warpgroup matmul and the producer/consumer pattern that keeps the tensor cores saturated on Hopper.

articles.title: Beating cuBLAS on an H100
articles.blurb: A full worklog (after hamzaelshafie & cudaforfun) that assembles TMA + wgmma + warp specialization into a library-beating GEMM.

articles.title: Blackwell: tcgen05 & tensor memory
articles.blurb: The 5th-gen tensor cores, the new Tensor Memory (TMEM), and CTA pairs — a different machine to write for.

articles.title: NVFP4 & microscaling formats
articles.blurb: e2m1 with FP8 block scales, and the hackathon journey from a 2000µs to a 22µs FP4 GEMV.

articles.title: DeepSeek's open kernels: FlashMLA & DeepGEMM
articles.blurb: MLA decode, FP8 MoE GEMMs and the DeepGEMM backend — production kernels behind a frontier model.

articles.title: DSpark: speculative decoding as a kernel problem
articles.blurb: V4-Pro's speculative-decoding module — draft passes, parallel verification, acceptance kernels, and CSA/HCA sparse attention.

articles.title: CUTLASS the hard way
articles.blurb: From a naive GEMM to a real CUTLASS kernel (after kapilsh) — reading library-grade warptiling with our own vocabulary.

articles.title: CuTe, the DSL landscape & Triton
articles.blurb: Layouts and tensors in CuTe, Triton in 40 lines, TileLang — the abstractions above raw CUDA and when to use them.

articles.title: Debugging kernels: the vLLM workflow
articles.blurb: compute-sanitizer, user-triggered core dumps, cuda-gdb, -lineinfo and nvdisasm — finding the illegal access at 3am.

sections.title: AI × Kernels
sections.blurb: The newest frontier: can models write the kernels? KernelBench, test-time search, RL, and the honest picture of where AI-generated kernels win and where they still fail.

articles.title: KernelBench & measuring AI kernels
articles.blurb: The benchmark, the fast_p metric, and why 'correct and faster than PyTorch' is a surprisingly hard bar.

articles.title: Monkeys & search: test-time scaling
articles.blurb: Sampling 100 kernels, iterative feedback, and evolutionary/tree search — how DeepSeek-V3 went 4% → 72%.

articles.title: The CRFM experiments
articles.blurb: Natural-language optimization ideas + parallel branching: LayerNorm at 484% of PyTorch — and FlashAttention at 9%.

articles.title: Kevin, RL & what's still human
articles.blurb: Multi-turn RL with verifiable rewards, the KernelBook dataset, and why the human+AI+profiler loop is the real answer.
